metadata.summary: Single ingress handling routing, TLS, auth, rate limiting, CORS, and observability for multi-service backends via declarative GitOps config, stateless design, and per-route timeouts.

content.title: API Gateway Patterns
content.summary: Centralizing cross-cutting concerns at the gateway removes the need for every service to reimplement JWT validation, rate limits, and CORS headers. GitOps of gateway config (decK sync, terraform apply) prevents config drift that causes silent production incidents. Stateless gateways scale horizontally; stateful ones create sticky-session failures.

why.p: An API gateway is a single ingress that handles routing, TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting, CORS, request transformation, and observability for multiple backend services. Centralizing cross-cutting concerns at the gateway removes the need for every service to reimplement JWT validation, rate limits, and CORS headers. GitOps of gateway config (decK sync, terraform apply) prevents config drift that causes silent production incidents. Stateless gateways scale horizontally; stateful ones create sticky-session failures.

list.item: Multi-service backends needing one stable public URL regardless of internal topology
Mobile + Web + Partner clients with different payload shapes (BFF pattern)
Enforcing org-wide policies (mTLS, JWT, audit logging) without per-service duplication
Strangler-fig migrations routing legacy paths to old service, new paths to new service
Single-service apps where Nginx + app middleware suffice — full gateway is operational overhead
Latency-critical hot paths (real-time bidding, HFT) — prefer sidecar/mesh direct path
Greenfield prototypes — start with a reverse proxy, add gateway when second service ships
Pure static asset delivery (CDN handles routing, caching, TLS)

section.name: core-functions
section.p: The gateway handles: routing (direct requests to the correct upstream service), load balancing (distribute traffic across service instances with weights), authentication (validate JWT signatures, expiry, audience before proxying), rate limiting (protect services from overload; use Redis-backed counters in multi-replica deployments), CORS (centralized preflight handling so services don't duplicate it), request transformation (inject X-Request-ID, strip downstream Authorization if needed), SSL/TLS termination (handle HTTPS at the edge; re-encrypt to backends for sensitive routes), and observability (emit Prometheus metrics + structured access logs with traceparent propagation).

list.item: Routing — direct requests to the correct upstream service
Load balancing — distribute traffic across service instances with weights
Authentication — validate JWT signatures, expiry, audience before proxying
Rate limiting — protect services from overload; use Redis-backed counters in multi-replica deployments
CORS — centralized preflight handling so services don't duplicate it
Request transformation — inject X-Request-ID, strip downstream Authorization if needed
SSL/TLS termination — handle HTTPS at the edge; re-encrypt to backends for sensitive routes
Observability — emit Prometheus metrics + structured access logs with traceparent propagation

section.name: bff-composition-pattern
section.p: In BFF (Backend for Frontend) handlers that fan out to multiple upstream services, use asyncio.gather with per-call timeouts and circuit breakers. Never run upstream calls serially. Serial calls multiply latency: 3 × 500ms = 1500ms. asyncio.gather runs them concurrently; per-call timeout bounds the worst case.

rule.statement: In BFF (Backend for Frontend) handlers that fan out to multiple upstream services, use asyncio.gather with per-call timeouts and circuit breakers. Never run upstream calls serially.
rule.rationale: Serial calls multiply latency: 3 × 500ms = 1500ms. asyncio.gather runs them concurrently; per-call timeout bounds the worst case.

rule.statement: Choose Kong + decK for self-hosted multi-service production workloads. Use Nginx as a simpler gateway for 1–2 services. Use AWS API Gateway only when already in the AWS ecosystem and Lambda/VPC integration is needed.
rule.rationale: Kong has a plugin ecosystem and declarative GitOps via decK. Nginx suffices for simple reverse-proxy use cases without plugin overhead.

rule.statement: All gateway config must be declarative and version-controlled in the repo. No UI-driven edits. Apply via decK sync, terraform apply, or serverless deploy.
rule.rationale: Manual UI edits diverge from git state; the next deploy overwrites them silently, causing incidents.

rule.statement: Never store secrets inline in gateway YAML. Reference vault paths (Kong vault refs, AWS Secrets Manager ARNs, or environment variable refs).
rule.rationale: Inline secrets committed to git are irrevocable; even after removal, git history retains them.

section.name: policy-rules
section.p: Every public route must carry: auth plugin (jwt/oauth), rate-limiting (Redis-backed), CORS, X-Request-ID injection, and observability (Prometheus/access log). Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry only. Fine-grained authorization (scope, role, resource ownership) stays in the service. Set explicit connect_timeout, read_timeout, and write_timeout for every upstream route. Gateway timeout must be greater than expected service p99 + jitter.

rule.statement: Every public route must carry: auth plugin (jwt/oauth), rate-limiting (Redis-backed), CORS, X-Request-ID injection, and observability (Prometheus/access log).
rule.rationale: Missing rate-limiting on one public route is the DoS entry point attackers find first.

rule.statement: Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry only. Fine-grained authorization (scope, role, resource ownership) stays in the service.
rule.rationale: Duplicating fine-grained auth in the gateway creates divergent logic that disagreements cause security gaps.

rule.statement: Set explicit connect_timeout, read_timeout, and write_timeout for every upstream route. Gateway timeout must be greater than expected service p99 + jitter.
rule.rationale: Missing timeouts lead to hung connections and gateway thread starvation under slow upstreams.

antipattern.description: Rate-limiting plugin configured with in-memory policy in a multi-replica gateway deployment.
antipattern.reason: Each replica tracks its own counter; effective limit = N × configured limit. Always use Redis policy for shared state.

template.purpose: Kong declarative config: services, routes, JWT + rate-limit + CORS plugins, request-id injection
Nginx reverse proxy with upstream weighting, rate limiting, CORS, health check
CI script: fails if any public route is missing auth + rate-limit + CORS plugins
